evidence: src/lib/network/sdk.ts has mutable module-level state: 'export let RPC_URL', 'export let SUPERCOLONY_API', 'let logAgentName = process.env.AGENT_NAME', 'let resolvedAlgorithm', 'let resolvedDualSign' — these are mutated by applyConfigOverrides() which is called from loadMnemonic()
sdk.ts has an import-time side effect: 'if (!globalThis.crypto) { (globalThis as any).crypto = webcrypto; }' — polyfill runs when any file imports sdk.ts
sdk.ts XDG_CREDENTIALS path is computed at import time via resolve(homedir(), ...) — this is a constant, not mutable, so it's acceptable

src/lib/pipeline/observe.ts uses module-level mutable state (_agentName, _sessionNumber, _logPath, _currentPhase) but follows init/use pattern via initObserver() — callers must call initObserver() before observe() works
observe.ts gracefully handles uninitialized state: 'if (!_logPath) return' — silent no-op before init, not a crash
src/lib/state.ts has DEFAULT_SESSIONS_DIR computed at import time but this is a constant — no concern
src/toolkit/tools/connect.ts explicitly avoids module-level side effects: 'Uses lazy SDK imports to avoid module-level side effects' — good pattern
The calibrate-plugin.ts uses factory injection to avoid circular init dependencies — createCalibrateBeforeSense(runTool) receives its dependency at runtime
No circular init dependencies detected — the one import cycle (extensions.ts → calibrate-plugin.ts) is a runtime cycle resolved via lazy loading
process.env.AGENT_NAME read at module level in sdk.ts — fragile in testing but only used for log labeling, not control flow

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase has one significant initialization coupling hotspot in sdk.ts, which combines mutable exported lets, a globalThis polyfill side effect, and environment variable reads at module scope. The mutable state (RPC_URL, SUPERCOLONY_API) is modified by loadMnemonic() side effects, creating implicit ordering dependencies — callers must ensure loadMnemonic() runs before reading the exported URLs. Other modules (observe.ts) use init/use patterns with graceful degradation, and connect.ts explicitly documents its avoidance of module-level side effects.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Score of 79.0 reflects a codebase where most modules avoid import-time coupling, but sdk.ts is a systemic concern — it's imported by 12 files and its mutable state mutation via loadMnemonic() creates hidden ordering dependencies. The globalThis polyfill is a minor concern (standard Node 18 practice). The observe.ts module-level state is properly gated. Anchored between 75 (one problematic module with wide blast radius) and 80 (mostly clean initialization).

issues.summary: sdk.ts exports mutable lets (RPC_URL, SUPERCOLONY_API) that are silently mutated by loadMnemonic() side effects
evidence: Line 25: 'export let RPC_URL = "https://demosnode.discus.sh/"' — default value overwritten by applyConfigOverrides()
Line 26: 'export let SUPERCOLONY_API = "https://www.supercolony.ai"' — same pattern
applyConfigOverrides() is called inside loadMnemonic() — any importer reading RPC_URL before loadMnemonic() gets the default, after gets the override
12 files import from sdk.ts — wide blast radius for stale-snapshot bugs
connectWallet() reads resolvedAlgorithm which is set by the same side-effecting path
issues.suggestion: Replace mutable module-level state with a config object returned by loadMnemonic() or a lazy getter. For example: export function getRpcUrl(): string { return _rpcUrl; } — or better, make loadMnemonic() return a config object { mnemonic, rpcUrl, apiUrl, algorithm, dualSign } and thread it to consumers explicitly.

issues.summary: sdk.ts has import-time globalThis.crypto polyfill that runs on any import
evidence: Lines 16-18: 'if (!globalThis.crypto) { (globalThis as any).crypto = webcrypto; }' — runs at import time
This is a common Node 18 pattern but it mutates global state as a side effect of importing a module
issues.suggestion: This is low severity for Node 18 compatibility. If targeting Node 20+, remove the polyfill. Otherwise, consider moving it to a dedicated setup/polyfill.ts imported explicitly at entry points.

issues.summary: observe.ts requires initObserver() to be called before observe() works, creating implicit boot-order dependency
evidence: Lines 85-88: module-level mutable state (_agentName, _sessionNumber, _logPath, _currentPhase) with defaults
Line 96: initObserver() must be called to set up _logPath — without it, observe() silently no-ops
The silent no-op behavior (line 144) is graceful but hides misconfiguration in tests
issues.suggestion: The current design is acceptable since observe is non-critical (best-effort logging). For stricter environments, consider a factory pattern: createObserver(agent, session) returning an Observer instance rather than relying on module-level state.

root_causes: sdk.ts was originally written as a singleton module for CLI tools where init ordering was implicit — the mutable lets pattern doesn't scale to a library consumed by diverse callers
likely_symptoms: observe_implicit_init_ordering is a mild version of the same singleton-module pattern

add.header: connect.ts explicitly avoids module-level side effects
add.description: The toolkit connect.ts documents 'Uses lazy SDK imports to avoid module-level side effects' — showing awareness of the issue and deliberate mitigation in newer code.

add.header: Factory injection pattern used for circular avoidance
add.description: calibrate-plugin.ts uses createCalibrateBeforeSense(runTool) factory pattern to break circular init dependencies between session-runner and plugins. This is a good pattern.

add.header: sdk.ts mutable exports create ordering dependencies
add.description: RPC_URL and SUPERCOLONY_API are exported as mutable lets, silently mutated by loadMnemonic(). Any importer reading them before wallet connection gets stale defaults. This is the primary init-coupling concern.
